commands.repair_reason: Protected maintainer label plus missing real behavior proof make this a human proof and maintainer-review follow-up, not an automated repair task.; Cleared: No concrete security or supply-chain concern found; the diff touches doctor command logic, tests, and docs without dependency, workflow, lockfile, secret, or external code-execution changes. commands.review_summary:
The PR routes doctor --lint core checks through ordered doctor contributions, narrows the broad structured repair bridge to non-core checks, adds structuredHealth contribution plumbing and tests, updates doctor docs, and removes the conversion-plan ledger.

PR surface: Source -139, Tests +61, Docs +2. Total -76 across 10 files.

Reproducibility: not applicable. this is a PR refactor/ordering change, not a standalone bug report. Source and tests show the intended path, but no real after-patch doctor CLI output is posted.
commands.review_followup: Protected maintainer label plus missing real behavior proof make this a human proof and maintainer-review follow-up, not an automated repair task.

target.title: Keep core doctor health in contribution order
target.body:
## Summary

This PR integrates structured health with the ordered core doctor checks. The goal is to make it cheap and safe to add structured findings, dry-run previews, and diff/effect reporting to existing doctor rules without moving those rules out of the doctor order that already works.

The model is:

- core doctor checks stay owned by `resolveDoctorHealthContributions()` / `createDoctorHealthContribution(...)`, which remains the source of truth for `openclaw doctor` and `openclaw doctor --fix` ordering
- core contributions can now declare `structuredHealth` directly; the factory derives `healthCheckIds` from those structured checks when possible
- if a contribution still has legacy `run`, that legacy path remains authoritative for normal `doctor` and `doctor --fix`
- if legacy `run` is omitted, `structuredHealth` owns the contribution execution path through the structured repair runner
- `doctor --lint` runs structured core checks in contribution order first, then appends non-core bundled/plugin registry checks
- the broad structured repair bridge is reserved for extension/plugin checks rather than becoming a second core repair runner
- the temporary doctor health conversion-plan ledger is removed because contribution ordering and contribution/core-check tests now own the invariant directly

That gives us the migration ladder we wanted: keep existing doctor repair behavior in place while adding structured lint/dry-run/diff data, then remove legacy `run` only when that specific core contribution is ready for structured health to take over fully.

## How A Core Rule Upgrades

Before an upgrade, a rule is just an ordered doctor contribution:

```ts
createDoctorHealthContribution({
  id: "doctor:some-rule",
  label: "Some rule",
  run: runSomeRuleHealth,
})
```

For the safe in-place upgrade, the rule keeps its old `run` and adds structured health beside it. Legacy `run` still owns real doctor behavior; structured health supplies lint/dry-run/diff/effect data:

```ts
createDoctorHealthContribution({
  id: "doctor:some-rule",
  label: "Some rule",
  run: runSomeRuleHealth,
  structuredHealth: someRuleStructuredHealth,
})
```

When the rule is ready to be fully structured, the legacy `run` can be removed and structured health owns the contribution execution path:

```ts
createDoctorHealthContribution({
  id: "doctor:some-rule",
  label: "Some rule",
  structuredHealth: someRuleStructuredHealth,
})
```

`structuredHealth` can be a split `detect` / `repair` check or a single structured `run` check. If it has repair behavior, it is expected to honor the existing dry-run/diff/effects conventions.

## Validation

- `git diff --check`
- `node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts src/flows/doctor-repair-flow.ts`
- `node_modules/.bin/oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts src/flows/doctor-repair-flow.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts src/flows/doctor-repair-flow.test.ts --reporter=dot` (2 files, 35 tests)
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --noEmit --incremental false --pretty false`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --noEmit --incremental false --pretty false`
- Earlier full branch proof before this refinement: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts src/commands/doctor-lint.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-browser-residue-check.test.ts src/flows/doctor-lint-flow.test.ts src/flows/doctor-repair-flow.test.ts --reporter=dot` (6 files, 50 tests)
- Earlier branch proof: `bash /mnt/c/src/claws-hapi/.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base origin/main` clean after removing the conversion ledger
- Earlier branch proof: `codex review --base origin/main` clean

## Updated In-Place Upgrade Stack

These are the doctor PRs already updated to take advantage of this model:

- #85566 - shell completion: structured lint health is owned by the existing `doctor:shell-completion` contribution.
- #84290 - UI freshness: structured lint/dry-run effects report missing/stale Control UI assets, while real build/rebuild stays on the existing doctor UI freshness path.
- #84366 - session locks: structured lint/dry-run effects report stale lock cleanup, while real cleanup stays on the existing session-lock doctor path.

The remaining older doctor repair PRs should be rebased onto this PR before they are presented as part of the same in-place upgrade series.
target.author: giodl73-repo
